prompt: Which sentence about **Tier Level 4** of a datacenter is **not** correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All cooling equipment is independently dual-powered

choices.label: B
choices.text: Single non-redundant distribution path serving the IT equipment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Meets or exceeds all Tier 3 requirements

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fault-tolerant site infrastructure with availability of 99.995%

solution: Tier 4 is the highest datacenter classification: **fault tolerant**, fully redundant (2N+1), with multiple independent and physically isolated distribution paths and dual-powered cooling, reaching an availability of ~99.995% (about 26 min downtime/year), and it meets or exceeds all lower-tier requirements. A **single non-redundant distribution path** (option B) describes **Tier 1** (basic capacity), not Tier 4, so B is the statement that is **not** correct. The other three statements (A, C, D) are all true of Tier 4.

prompt: In the context of data centers, **scalability** is the ability for the infrastructure ______ without cost, efficiency, and reliability being compromised. Select the correct item to be included in the previous sentence.

choices.label: A
choices.text: to be enlarged or to handle an increment of the input requests

choices.label: B
choices.text: to run in a larger building

solution: Scalability is the capability of a system/infrastructure to **grow (be enlarged) and to handle an increasing amount of input requests/work** while keeping cost, efficiency and reliability under control. Running in a larger building (B) or improving cooling (D) are unrelated physical/operational aspects, so the correct completion is A.

prompt: Which is the most appropriate definition for "**fault**"?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Any library inconsistency in the Operating System

choices.label: B
choices.text: Any defect in the silicon caused by dust particles during manufacturing

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any inappropriate usage of the system

choices.label: D
choices.text: Any event interfering with the nominal behavior of the system

solution: In the dependability chain **fault → error → failure**, a *fault* is the cause: any defect/anomalous condition or event that can perturb the system from its nominal behaviour. Options A and B are far too narrow (one specific kind of fault each), and C describes a misuse, not a fault. The broad, course-level definition is **any event interfering with the nominal behavior of the system** (D).

prompt: One of these levels is **NOT** part of a typical three-layers network architecture of a data-center?
solution: The classic three-tier datacenter network architecture is built from the **Access** layer (top-of-rack / edge switches connecting servers), the **Aggregation (Distribution)** layer, and the **Core** layer (high-speed backbone). **Cloud** is not a layer of this physical network topology, so D is the one that does not belong.

prompt: All faults occurring in a system cause a failure of the system.

subStatements.statement: All faults occurring in a system cause a failure of the system.
subStatements.motivation: False. In the fault → error → failure chain, a fault only *potentially* leads to a failure. A fault may stay dormant (never activated), or it may be masked/tolerated by redundancy and fault-tolerance mechanisms so it never propagates to an error or to a service failure. Hence not every fault causes a failure.

prompt: In datacenter storage context, DAS means Distributed Application Storage.

subStatements.statement: In datacenter storage context, DAS means Distributed Application Storage.
subStatements.motivation: False. DAS stands for **Direct Attached Storage** — storage directly attached to a single server (via SAS/SATA/SCSI) with no network in between — as opposed to NAS (Network Attached Storage) and SAN (Storage Area Network). It is not 'Distributed Application Storage'.

prompt: In the context of rack servers, 1U is 44.45 cm.

subStatements.statement: In the context of rack servers, 1U is 44.45 cm.
subStatements.motivation: False. One rack unit (1U) is defined as **1.75 inches = 4.445 cm**, not 44.45 cm. The value 44.45 cm corresponds to 10U (and is off by a factor of ten from the correct 4.445 cm).

prompt: In a reliability block diagram, adding components in series decreases the system reliability with respect to the reliability of the single component.

subStatements.statement: In a reliability block diagram, adding components in series decreases the system reliability with respect to the reliability of the single component.
subStatements.motivation: True. For a series RBD the system works only if **all** components work, so $R_{sys}=\prod_i R_i$. Since each $R_i\in[0,1]$, the product is $\le$ any individual $R_i$; adding more series components multiplies by a factor $\le 1$, so overall reliability decreases relative to a single component.

prompt: Warehouse-scale computers run a large number of very small applications.

subStatements.statement: Warehouse-scale computers run a large number of very small applications.
subStatements.motivation: False. Warehouse-Scale Computers (WSCs) are designed to run a **small number of very large applications** (e.g. web search, mail, maps) whose workload is spread across tens of thousands of machines that act as a single computer — the opposite of many tiny independent applications.

prompt: The analysis of the failure behavior of a two-component system reveals that the system is down only when **both** its components are down. The two components A and B have the following characteristics: $MTTF_A = 200\,\text{days}$, $MTTR_A = 4\,\text{days}$, $MTTF_B = 120\,\text{days}$, $MTTR_B = 2\,\text{days}$. What is the **reliability** of the system at $t = 50$ days?
solution:
The system is down only when **both** components are down, so from a reliability standpoint A and B are in **parallel** (the system works if at least one component works). Reliability does **not** depend on MTTR (repair); the MTTR values are distractors.

**Per-component reliability (exponential model, $\lambda = 1/MTTF$):**
$$R_A(t) = e^{-t/MTTF_A} = e^{-50/200} = e^{-0.25} = 0.778801$$
$$R_B(t) = e^{-t/MTTF_B} = e^{-50/120} = e^{-0.416667} = 0.659240$$

**Parallel combination:**
$$R_{sys}(t) = 1 - \big(1-R_A\big)\big(1-R_B\big)$$
$$R_{sys}(50) = 1 - (1-0.778801)(1-0.659240) = 1 - (0.221199)(0.340760)$$
$$= 1 - 0.075372 = 0.924628$$

**FINAL RESULT:** $R_{sys}(50\,\text{days}) \approx 0.9246 = \mathbf{92.46\%}$

prompt: You are in charge of designing a computer system to control the power grid for the NorthWest region of Italy. A period with the system unavailable creates serious problems (lights off, loss of productivity, possible civil disorder). You set the goal of a system with an **availability greater than 99.9999%**. You must use building-block units with $MTTF = 1000$ hours and $MTTR = 12$ min. How many **parallel** instances must you arrange to meet the goal? Use at least 9 decimal digits for all intermediate calculations.
solution:
**Single-unit availability** ($MTTR = 12\,\text{min} = 0.2\,\text{h}$):
$$A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} = \frac{1000}{1000.2} = 0.999800040$$

**Single-unit unavailability:**
$$U = 1 - A = 0.000199960$$

**Parallel of $n$ identical units** is unavailable only if all $n$ are down:
$$U_{sys}(n) = U^{\,n}, \qquad A_{sys}(n) = 1 - U^{\,n}$$

Goal: $A_{sys} > 0.999999 \;\Rightarrow\; U^{\,n} < 10^{-6}$.

- $n = 1$: $U^1 = 1.99960\times10^{-4}$ → $A = 0.999800040$ ❌
- $n = 2$: $U^2 = (0.000199960)^2 = 3.99840\times10^{-8}$ → $A = 0.999999960$ ✓ (> 99.9999%)

**FINAL RESULT:** $n = \mathbf{2}$ parallel instances.

prompt: Consider an HDD with a data transfer rate of 10 MB/s, a rotation speed of 7500 RPM, a mean seek time of 6 ms, and a negligible controller overhead. What is the minimum **locality** required to achieve a mean I/O service time of 2.20 ms to transfer a sector of 4 KB?
solution:
**I/O service time model (with locality $L$):**
$$T_{I/O} = (1-L)\,(T_{seek} + T_{rot}) + T_{transfer} + T_{overhead}$$
where locality $L\in[0,1]$ reduces the mechanical positioning time (seek + rotational latency); $T_{overhead}=0$ (negligible).

**Rotational latency** (half a revolution at 7500 RPM):
$$T_{rot} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{60}{7500}\,\text{s} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot 8\,\text{ms} = 4\,\text{ms}$$

**Transfer time** of one 4 KB sector at 10 MB/s:
$$T_{transfer} = \frac{4\,\text{KB}}{10\,\text{MB/s}} = \frac{4\times10^{3}}{10\times10^{6}}\,\text{s} = 0.4\,\text{ms}$$

**Solve for $L$** with $T_{seek}=6$ ms:
$$2.20 = (1-L)\,(6 + 4) + 0.4$$
$$2.20 - 0.4 = (1-L)\cdot 10 \;\Rightarrow\; 1.8 = 10\,(1-L)$$
$$1-L = 0.18 \;\Rightarrow\; L = 0.82$$

**FINAL RESULT:** minimum locality $L = \mathbf{0.82 = 82.00\%}$.

prompt: A RAID 5 system uses four 2 TB disks to store data and the required parity bits. Each disk has a Sequential Access Speed (throughput) of 50 MB/s and a Random Access Speed (throughput) of 5 MB/s. What is the expected throughput of the RAID 5 considering a **random write** pattern?
solution:
RAID 5 suffers the well-known **small (random) write penalty**: to update a block, the controller must perform a **read-modify-write** that involves 4 disk operations — read the old data block, read the old parity, then write the new data block and write the new parity.

For a **random** write pattern the limiting factor is the **random access speed of the disks**, not the sequential one. Each random write must read+write the targeted data disk and the parity disk; these dependent random operations cap the sustained throughput at the random throughput of a single disk:
$$X_{random\;write} = \text{Random Access Speed of one disk} = 5\,\text{MB/s}$$

The extra parity read/write and the data read/write are serialized on a per-update basis, so the array does not deliver more useful random-write bandwidth than one disk's random speed.

**FINAL RESULT:** expected RAID 5 random-write throughput $= \mathbf{5\,\text{MB/s}}$.

prompt: Consider a RAID 0+1 setup with 8 disks, each with an $MTTF = 750$ days and an $MTTR = 12$ days. Consider a single-mirror case for the RAID 1 part. What is the **MTTF** of the storage infrastructure?
solution:
**RAID 0+1** = a mirror (RAID 1) of two striped sets (RAID 0). With $n$ total disks (two halves of $n/2$ each), the array survives as long as not both mirrored halves have lost a disk. Using the standard course formula for RAID 0+1 MTTF:
$$MTTF_{RAID\,0+1} = \frac{MTTF^2 \cdot 2}{n^2 \cdot MTTR}$$

**Substitute** $MTTF = 750$ days, $MTTR = 12$ days, $n = 8$:
$$MTTF_{RAID\,0+1} = \frac{750^2 \cdot 2}{8^2 \cdot 12} = \frac{562500 \cdot 2}{64 \cdot 12} = \frac{1\,125\,000}{768}$$
$$= 1464.84\,\text{days}$$

**FINAL RESULT:** $MTTF \approx \mathbf{1465\,\text{days}}$.

prompt: Your system initially includes one CPU and one disk and serves 20 users with a 20 s think time. The CPU demand is $D_{CPU} = 100\,\text{ms}$ and the disk demand is $D_{DISK} = 300\,\text{ms}$. How many **disks** do you need to install so that the **response-time lower bound** is lower than 500 ms? (Assume you can evenly split the disk demand among all the disks used.)
solution:
**Response-time lower bound (asymptotic bounds).** The smallest possible response time of a closed system is its total **service demand** $D$ (achieved at low load):
$$R \ge D = \sum_k D_k$$

If the disk demand is split evenly across $k$ disks, each disk station has demand $D_{DISK}/k$, but the **total** disk work is unchanged, so the sum of demands stays:
$$D = D_{CPU} + k\cdot\frac{D_{DISK}}{k} = D_{CPU} + D_{DISK} = 100 + 300 = 400\,\text{ms}$$

The response-time lower bound is therefore $R_{min} = D = 400\,\text{ms}$ **regardless** of how many disks are used (splitting only lowers the per-station demand / raises the saturation point, it does not change the total demand). Since
$$400\,\text{ms} < 500\,\text{ms},$$
the requirement is already satisfied with the original single disk; no additional disks are needed.

**FINAL RESULT:** $\mathbf{1\ disk}$ (the existing one) is sufficient.

prompt: Consider the same system and situation as in the previous question. Knowing that the system throughput is $X = 0.8\,\text{req/s}$, what is the system response time $R_{Sys}$?
solution:
**Interactive Response Time Law** for a closed system with $N$ users and think time $Z$:
$$N = X\,(R_{Sys} + Z) \;\Rightarrow\; R_{Sys} = \frac{N}{X} - Z$$

**Substitute** $N = 20$ users, $X = 0.8\,\text{req/s}$, $Z = 20\,\text{s}$:
$$R_{Sys} = \frac{20}{0.8} - 20 = 25 - 20 = 5\,\text{s}$$

**FINAL RESULT:** $R_{Sys} = \mathbf{5\,\text{s}}$.

prompt: Classify the various types of clouds, while providing a short description of them.
solution:
Clouds are classified along **two orthogonal dimensions**: the *deployment model* (who owns/uses the infrastructure) and the *service model* (what layer of the stack is offered).

**1. Deployment models**
- **Public cloud** — infrastructure owned and operated by a third-party provider (e.g. AWS, Azure, GCP) and shared among many tenants over the Internet (multi-tenant). Pay-per-use, high elasticity, no capital expenditure; less control and possible data-governance concerns.
- **Private cloud** — cloud infrastructure dedicated to a single organization, hosted on-premises or by a third party. Greater control, security and compliance, but higher cost and the organization bears capacity planning.
- **Community cloud** — shared by several organizations with common concerns (e.g. same regulatory or mission requirements); costs and governance are shared among the community.
- **Hybrid cloud** — a combination of two or more of the above (typically private + public), bound together to allow data/application portability; enables *cloud bursting* (overflow peak load from private to public) while keeping sensitive workloads private.

**2. Service models (the cloud 'stack')**
- **IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)** — provides virtualized fundamental resources: compute (VMs), storage, network. The user manages OS, runtime and applications; the provider manages the physical hardware and virtualization (e.g. AWS EC2).
- **PaaS (Platform as a Service)** — provides a managed application platform (runtime, middleware, databases, OS). The user only deploys and manages their application/code; the provider manages everything below (e.g. Google App Engine, Azure App Service).
- **SaaS (Software as a Service)** — provides a complete, ready-to-use application delivered over the network; the user only configures and uses it, while the provider manages the entire stack (e.g. Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce).

Going IaaS → PaaS → SaaS, the **management responsibility shifts from the user to the provider** and the level of abstraction increases. (More specialized models also exist, e.g. *FaaS / serverless* and *ML-as-a-Service*, where increasingly fine-grained capabilities are billed on a pure usage basis.)
